volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 1.2.144 - Section 1.4.243)

sections.label: Section 1.2.144
sections.original: Nullumst periclum te hinc ire inpransum domum :
sections.translation: There ’ s no risk then in your betaking yourself hence home without your breakfast .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Cererem te meliust quam Venerem sectarier :
sections.translation: It ’ s better for you to be a waiter upon Ceres than upon Venus ; the latter attends to love , Ceres attends to wheat .

sections.label: Section 1.2.147
sections.original: Deludificavit me illic homo indignis modis .
sections.translation: This fellow has been making sport of me in a digraceful manner .

sections.label: Section 1.2.148
sections.original: Pro di immortales , quid illuc est , Sceparnio ,
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods , Sceparnio , what means those people near the sea-shore ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.149b
sections.original: Vt mea est opinio ,
sections.translation: According to my notion ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: propter viam illi sunt vocati ad prandium .
sections.translation: they ’ ve been invited to a parting breakfast [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.151
sections.original: Qui ?
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.151b
sections.original: Quia post cenam , credo , laverunt heri .
sections.translation: Why , because , after dinner , I fancy , they yesterday washed themselves clean ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.152
sections.original: Confracta navis in mari est illis .
sections.translation: their ship has gone to pieces out at sea .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152b
sections.original: Ita est .
sections.translation: Such is the fact .

sections.label: Section 1.2.153
sections.original: at hercle nobis villa in terra et tegulae .
sections.translation: But , I ’ faith , on dry land our cottage and tiles have done the same .

sections.label: Section 1.2.153b
sections.original: Hui ,
sections.translation: Oh dear ! what unfortunate creatures you are ; ( to SCEPARNIO ) how the shipwrecked people are swimming .

sections.label: Section 1.2.156
sections.original: Vbi sunt i homines , obsecro ?
sections.translation: Prithee , where are these people ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.156b
sections.original: Hac ad dexteram
sections.translation: This way , to the right ; don ’ t you see them near the shore ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.157b
sections.original: Video . sequimini .
sections.translation: I see them ; ( to his FRIENDS ) follow me . I only wish it may be he that I ’ m seeking , that most accursed fellow . ( To DAEMONES and SCEPARNIO .) Fare you well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.159b
sections.original: Si non moneas , nosmet meminimus .
sections.translation: If you hadn ’ t put us in mind , we should have thought of that ourselves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.160
sections.original: sed , o Palaemon , sancte Neptuni comes ,
sections.translation: But , O Palaemon [*] , hallowed associate of Neptune , who art said to be the partner of Hercules , what shocking thing do I see ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.162b
sections.original: Quid vides ?
sections.translation: What do you see ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.162c
sections.original: Mulierculas
sections.translation: I see two young women sitting in a boat alone . How the poor things are being tossed about That ’ s good , that ’ s good , well done .

sections.label: Section 1.2.165
sections.original: ab saxo avortit fluctus ad litus scapham ,
sections.translation: The surge is driving the boat away from the rock towards the shore . Not a pilot could have ever done it better . I don ’ t think that I ever saw billows more huge . They are saved , if they can escape those waves . Now , now ’ s the danger ; it has sent one overboard !

sections.label: Section 1.2.170
sections.original: at in vadost , iam facile enabit . eugepae ,
sections.translation: Still , she ’ s in a shallow place ; she ’ ll easily wade through it now . O capital !

sections.label: Section 1.2.171
sections.original: viden alteram illam ut fluctus eiecit foras ?
sections.translation: See you that one whom the waves have thrown out of the boat ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.172
sections.original: surrexit , horsum se capessit . salva res .
sections.translation: now she ’ s safe ; she has escaped from the water ; she ’ s now on shore . But that other one has now sprung towards the land from the boat — from her alarm she has fallen into the waves upon her knees .

sections.label: Section 1.2.175
sections.original: salvast , evasit ex aqua . iam in litore est .
sections.translation: She has got up again ; if she takes this direction , the matter ’ s safe ; ( a pause ) but she has taken to the right , to utter destruction . Ah , she will be wandering all the day —

sections.label: Section 1.2.177b
sections.original: Quid id refert tua ?
sections.translation: What signifies that to you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.178
sections.original: Si ad saxum quo capessit , ea deorsum cadit ,
sections.translation: If she should fall down from that rock towards which she is wending her way ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.179_180
sections.original: errationis fecerit compendium .
sections.translation: she ’ ll be putting a period to her wandering .

sections.label: Section 1.2.181
sections.original: Si tu de illarum cenaturus vesperi es ,
sections.translation: If you are about to dine this evening at their expense , I think you may then be concerned for them , Sceparnio ; if you are going to eat at my house , I wish your services to be devoted to myself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.184
sections.original: Bonum aequomque oras .
sections.translation: You ask what ’ s good and proper .

sections.label: Section 1.2.184b
sections.original: Sequere me hac ergo .—
sections.translation: Then follow me this way .

sections.label: Section 1.2.184c
sections.original: Sequor .—
sections.translation: I follow [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.185
sections.original: Nimio hóminum fortunaé minus miseraé memorantur ,
sections.translation: By heavens , the mishaps of mortals are spoken of as much less bitter than ⋯ the sharp pangs that are inflicted in the experience of them ⋯ Has this then pleased the Deity , that I , clad in this guise , should , in my terror , be cast upon a spot unknown ? Shall I then declare that I have been born to this wretched lot ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.190
sections.original: hancíne ego partem cápio ob píetatem praecípuam ?
sections.translation: Do I receive this meed in return for my exemplary piety ? For to me it would not prove a hardship to endure this laborious lot , if I had conducted myself undutifully towards my parents or the Gods ; but if studiously I have exerted myself to beware of that , then , unduly and unjustly ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.195
sections.original: datis , dí ; nam quid habebúnt sibi signi ímpii posthac ,
sections.translation: Deities , you send upon me this . For what henceforth shall the glaringly impious receive , if after this fashion you pay honor to the guiltless ? But if I knew that I or my parents had done anything wicked , now should I have grieved the less . But the wickedness of this master of mine is pressing hard upon me , his impiety is causing my woes ; everything has he lost in the sea ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.200
sections.original: haéc bonorum éius sunt réliquiae ; étiam quae simul
sections.translation: these are the remains ( looking at her dress ) of his property . Even she , who was carried together with me in the boat , was washed out by the violence of the waves ; I am now alone . If she at least [*] had been saved for me , through her aid my affliction here would have been lighter to me . Now , what hope or aid or what counsel shall I receive ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.205
sections.original: ita híc sola sólis locis compotita sum .
sections.translation: a spot so lonesome here have I lighted upon alone ? Here are the rocks , here roars the sea , and not one individual comes across my path . This dress that I am clothed in forms all my riches quite entirely ; nor know I with what food or roof I am to be provided . What hope have I through which to desire to live ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.210
sections.original: néc loci gnára sum , néc vidi aut híc fui .
sections.translation: Neither am I acquainted with the place , nor was I ever here before . At least I could have wished for some one who would point out to me either a road or a path from these spots ; so much am I now at a loss for advice whether to go this way or that ; neither , indeed , do I see [*] anywhere near here a cultivated spot .

sections.label: Section 1.3.215
sections.original: álgor , errór , pavor , me ómnia tenent .
sections.translation: Cold , distraction , and alarm , have taken possession of all my limbs . My parents , you know not of this , that I am now thus wretched ; I that was born a woman entirely free , was so to no purpose . Am I at all the less in servitude now , than if I had been born a slave ? And never in any way has it been a profit to those who for their own sakes reared me up .

sections.label: Section 1.4.220
sections.original: Quid míhi meliust , quid mágis in remst , quam a córpore vitam ut sécludam ?
sections.translation: What is there better for me , what more to my advantage , than to shut out life from my body ? So wretched am I in my existence , and so many deadening cares are there in my breast ; so despicable is my lot ; I care not for my life ; I have lost the hope with which I used to comfort myself . All places have I now rambled about , and through each covert spot have I crawled along , to seek my fellow-slave with voice , eyes , ears , that I might trace her out .

sections.label: Section 1.4.225
sections.original: neque eam úsquam invenio néque quo eam neque quá quaeram consúltumst ,
sections.translation: And still I find her nowhere , nor have I yet determined whither to go , nor where to seek her , nor , in the meantime , do I find any person here to give me an answer , of whom I might make enquiry . No place , too , is there on earth more solitary than are these spots and this locality . And yet , if she lives , never while I exist will I cease before I discover her alive .

sections.label: Section 1.4.229
sections.original: Quóianam vox míhi prope hic sonat ?
sections.translation: Whose voice is it that sounds close by me here ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.230
sections.original: Pértimui , quis híc loquitur prope ?
sections.translation: I am alarmed . Who ’ s speaking near me ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.231
sections.original: Spés bona , obsecro , súbventa mihi ,
sections.translation: Prithee , kind Hope , do come to my aid .

sections.label: Section 1.4.232
sections.original: éxime ex hóc miseram metu .
sections.translation: Will you not rescue wretched me from this alarm ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.233
sections.original: Cérto vox múliebris aúris tetigít meas .
sections.translation: It ’ s a woman : a woman ’ s voice reaches my ears .

sections.label: Section 1.4.234
sections.original: Múlier est , múliebris vóx mi ad aurís venit .
sections.translation: Surely a woman ’ s voice reached my ears .

sections.label: Section 1.4.235
sections.original: num Ámpelisca óbsecro est ?
sections.translation: Prithee is it Ampelisca ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.235b
sections.original: Tén , Palaestra , aúdio ?
sections.translation: Is it you , Palaestra , that I hear ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.236
sections.original: Quín voco , ut me aúdiat , nómine illám suo ?
sections.translation: But why don ’ t I call her by her own name , that she may hear me ? ( With a loud voice .) Ampelisca !

sections.label: Section 1.4.237b
sections.original: Hém quis est ?
sections.translation: Ha ! who ’ s that ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.237c
sections.original: Ego , Palaestra ⋯
sections.translation: ’ Tis I .

sections.label: Section 1.4.238
sections.original: Díc ubi es ?
sections.translation: Tell me where [*] you are .

sections.label: Section 1.4.238b
sections.original: Pól ego nunc ín malis plúrimis .
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ m now in the midst of a multitude of woes .

sections.label: Section 1.4.239
sections.original: Sócia sum , néc minor párs meast quam tua .
sections.translation: I am your partner ; and no less is my own share than yours .

sections.label: Section 1.4.240
sections.original: séd videre éxpeto té .
sections.translation: But I long to see you .

sections.label: Section 1.4.240b
sections.original: Mihi es aémula .
sections.translation: In that wish you are my rival .

sections.label: Section 1.4.241
sections.original: Cónsequamúr gradu vócem . ubi es ?
sections.translation: Let ’ s follow our voices with our steps ; where are you ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.241b
sections.original: Écce me .
sections.translation: See , here am I . Step onward towards me , and come straight on to meet me .

sections.label: Section 1.4.242b
sections.original: Fit sédulo .
sections.translation: I ’ m doing so with care .

sections.label: Section 1.4.243
sections.original: Cédo manum .
sections.translation: Give me your hand .
